audience: Students, educators, and support adults learning remotely with Jumeirah College

hero.title: When remote school feels hard, start with the next small step.
hero.subtitle: You do not need to do this perfectly. Start with the help you need right now, do one small thing, and ask for help early if the day is becoming too much.
quickStartSteps: Choose the route that matches how you feel.
Do only the next step on the page.
If you are stuck, message a person early.
hero.quickStartNote: You do not need to solve the whole day at once. Just begin.

journeyCards.title: I am a student and need help now
journeyCards.description: Go straight to the support choices and find the next step, a script to use, or a person to contact.

journeyCards.title: I want to organise the day
journeyCards.description: See what matters today, how lessons work, and what helps remote learning stay manageable.

journeyCards.title: I need calm, comfort, or a person
journeyCards.description: Go straight to wellbeing contacts, quick reset steps, and trusted support when the day feels too big.

studentIntents.label: I feel overwhelmed
studentIntents.title: Make the day smaller first
studentIntents.reassurance: You do not need to solve everything at once. Start by shrinking the demand until the next step feels possible again.
studentIntents.nextStep: Stop looking at the full list, take one slow breath, and choose one task only for the next five minutes.
studentIntents.followUp: If it still feels too big after one small step, write down what comes next and contact a trusted adult instead of forcing your way through the whole lesson.
studentIntents.script: I am overwhelmed and need help to find the next small step.
visualItems: Look away from the full list.
Take one slow breath in and out.
Work for five minutes or message for help.

studentIntents.label: I can't get started
studentIntents.title: Start with the easiest visible action
studentIntents.reassurance: Starting is often the hardest part of remote learning. Momentum usually comes after the first small action, not before it.
studentIntents.nextStep: Put your phone away, close the tabs you do not need, open the lesson, and do only the first visible action.
studentIntents.followUp: If you still cannot begin after trying the first action, tell your teacher exactly what is stopping you and ask for a clearer first step.
studentIntents.script: I can see the lesson, but I cannot get started without one clearer first step.
visualItems: Write the first action in simple words.
studentIntents.contactValue: Use the live lesson, email, or Google Classroom comments

studentIntents.label: I'm confused
studentIntents.title: Ask for clarity before you push through
studentIntents.reassurance: Confusion does not mean you have failed. It usually means the route into the work needs to be clearer or smaller.
studentIntents.nextStep: Read the instruction out loud, underline one word you recognise, and ask what the first step is.
studentIntents.followUp: If reading is part of the barrier, ask for the task to be chunked, read aloud, or explained in another way.
studentIntents.script: I am not sure what the first step is. Could you make it smaller or clearer?
visualItems: Start with one part, not the whole task.

studentIntents.label: I need calm
studentIntents.title: Regulate before you rejoin
studentIntents.reassurance: If your body feels stressed, the first job is not productivity. The first job is helping yourself feel steady enough to continue.
studentIntents.nextStep: Slow your breathing, drop your shoulders, stand up for a moment, and choose one reset action before returning to the screen.
studentIntents.followUp: If calm does not come back quickly, use the wellbeing contacts instead of staying stuck on your own.
studentIntents.script: I need a short reset before I can rejoin properly.
visualItems: Look away from the screen or stretch for a moment.

studentIntents.label: I need a person
studentIntents.title: Go to a human support route quickly
studentIntents.reassurance: Some problems are too big for another checklist. If the barrier is emotional, practical, or building up fast, speak to a person early.
studentIntents.nextStep: Send a short message explaining what is wrong and whether the barrier is stress, understanding, time, access, or joining the lesson.
studentIntents.followUp: If one contact route is slow, use your subject teacher, tutor, pastoral lead, or support-for-learning contact next.
studentIntents.script: I need an adult to help me because this is affecting my day.
visualItems: Say what is wrong in one sentence.
Say what kind of barrier it is.
Follow up with another support contact if needed.
studentIntents.contactValue: Use your form tutor route first, then move to wellbeing or Support for Learning if needed

headings.journeyTitle: Choose the route that matches this moment
headings.journeyCaption: The site is organised by need, not by department. Start where the problem feels most immediate.
headings.intentPanelCaption: Pick the closest match. Each path gives one calm next step, a visual sequence, and a human support route if you need one.
headings.supportTitle: What do you need right now?
headings.supportCaption: Choose one common blocker. Read only that panel. Do the steps, then come back if you still need more help.
headings.supportTeamTitle: Need a person, not another checklist?
headings.supportTeamCaption: These are the fastest routes to real support for students.
headings.dayTitle: What matters in the school day
headings.phraseCaption: These scripts reduce the pressure of asking for help when a student is stuck or overwhelmed.
headings.routineCaption: Keep expectations clear, but keep the pathway calm and achievable.
headings.educatorCaption: Use these blocks when a learner is dysregulated, missing learning, or needs support adapted to additional needs.
headings.accessibilityCaption: Design support around reduced load, clearer sequencing, and flexible ways to access the same learning.
headings.wellbeingCaption: Keep calm, regulation, and visible human support close by when the learning day starts to feel too big.
headings.supportPageTitle: If the day is becoming too hard
headings.supportPageIntro: This section is for emotional support, regulation, and human contact points.
headings.adultSummaryTitle: Use this section after the student has found their immediate next step
headings.adultSummaryText: Keep the student journey primary. Use these adult guidance blocks to reduce barriers, make re-entry feel possible, and adapt support without increasing pressure.

helperCards.title: I feel overwhelmed
steps: Stop looking at everything else for a moment.
Take one slow breath in and one slow breath out.
Choose one task only.
Work for five minutes or ask for help.
helperCards.note: One small step is enough right now.

helperCards.title: I cannot focus
steps: Put your phone away.
Close tabs you do not need.
Set a five-minute timer.
Look only at the first instruction.
helperCards.note: The goal is not perfect focus. The goal is starting.

helperCards.title: I missed the start of the lesson
steps: Join or open the lesson now.
Read only the latest instruction first.
Email your teacher if you are late or lost.
Set a reminder for the next lesson.
helperCards.note: Rejoining matters more than catching up perfectly.

helperCards.title: I do not understand the task
steps: Read the instruction out loud.
Underline one word you recognise.
Ask what the first step is.
Start with one part, not the whole task.
helperCards.note: Understanding often starts after asking one clear question.

helperCards.title: I do not like reading on a screen
steps: Use text-to-speech or a read-aloud tool.
Read one sentence at a time.
Cover the rest of the text if that helps.
Ask for the task in smaller chunks.
helperCards.note: Listening and chunking are valid ways to access the work.
extras: Mac shortcut: Command plus F5 for VoiceOver
Windows shortcut: Control plus Windows key plus Enter for Narrator

helperCards.title: I need an adult to help me
steps: Subject teacher for classwork or lesson confusion.
Form tutor for daily support or if the day is going wrong.
Pastoral or wellbeing support if you feel stressed or overwhelmed.
Support for Learning if you need help understanding, organising, or getting started.
helperCards.note: You do not have to carry a difficult day on your own.

helperCards.title: I cannot manage my emails
steps: Check your email at set times, not constantly.
Look first for your teacher's name, deadlines, lesson links, or reminder words.
Reply straight away if it will take less than two minutes.
Star, archive, or close emails once you have dealt with them.
helperCards.note: You do not need to check emails all day.
extras: Try a two-minute inbox reset: star anything important, reply if it is quick, then archive or close the rest.
Ask your teacher if you are unsure which emails matter most.

helperCards.title: My eyes hurt
steps: Look away from the screen for 20 seconds.
Lower the screen brightness if it feels too strong.
Blink slowly and relax your eyes.
Stand up for a moment, then come back to one small task.
helperCards.note: A short screen break can help you reset.

helperCards.title: I cannot sit still
steps: Stand up and work if that helps.
Stretch for 30 seconds.
Move for two minutes.
Come back and do one task only.
helperCards.note: Movement can help your attention reset.

helperCards.title: I cannot join a lesson
steps: Email your teacher straight away.
Copy in your form tutor or pastoral lead if the issue is affecting the day.
Say what is wrong in one sentence.
Do not disappear. Let school know you are still here.
helperCards.note: Ask for help as soon as you can.

helperCards.title: I miss my friends
steps: Message a friend to check in.
Arrange a short call or study session if you can.
Tell your tutor if isolation is making the day harder.
Remember this is temporary and people still want to hear from you.
helperCards.note: Staying connected can make the day feel lighter.

dayExpectations: Join each lesson or task as close to the start time as you can.
Check Google Classroom and your timetable every day.
Attend form time and key updates when they are part of the day.
Complete work in manageable chunks, not all at once.
Ask for help before stress builds up.
Use reminders, notes, and routines to stay oriented.
lessonRules: Your work will usually be on Google Classroom.
Teachers may use Google Meet, email, chat, or posted tasks.
Read the latest instruction first before looking further back.
If you cannot join, contact the teacher and copy in the right pastoral lead.
Follow the timetable and use reminders if that helps.
onlineExpectations: Join lessons on time where possible.
Meet deadlines when you can, and communicate early if you cannot.
Keep your camera on when a teacher asks and school expectations require it.
Be kind to other people and to yourself.

supportPhrases.text: I am not sure what the first step is.
Could you explain that in a smaller chunk?
I am overwhelmed and need help getting started.
I cannot join right now but I am still here.

supportBullets: Break tasks into smaller parts before motivation drops.
Use the timetable as an orientation tool, not a pressure tool.
Prefer clear next steps over long explanations.
Make support visible before behaviour becomes the issue.
Check emails at set times instead of carrying them all day.
wellbeingHabits: Sleep and wake at a consistent time where possible.
Drink water and eat before concentration drops.
Move between lessons, even for one minute.
Build in time away from the screen after study blocks.
Stay in touch with friends or trusted adults when remote learning feels isolating.
wellbeingResetSteps: Look away from the screen or stretch for a moment.
Notice five things you can see.
Choose one small next action or one adult to contact.

wellbeingResources.title: Breathing video
wellbeingResources.description: A short mindfulness video to help a student regulate before rejoining learning.

wellbeingResources.title: Stretching reset
wellbeingResources.description: A quick physical reset when a learner has been sitting too long.

wellbeingResources.title: Stay connected
wellbeingResources.description: If you miss your friends, message one person or ask to join a short study call.

supportContacts.label: Support for Learning
supportContacts.value: f.nicconmara_jcd@gemsedu.com - for understanding work, organisation, and getting started

supportContacts.label: Form tutor
supportContacts.value: Use the school platform or agreed tutor contact route for daily support

supportContacts.label: Subject teacher
supportContacts.value: Use the live lesson, email, or Google Classroom comments for classwork and lesson help

supportContacts.label: Counselling Team
supportContacts.value: counselling_jcd@gemsedu.com - for stress, overwhelm, or emotional support

educatorCards.title: When a learner is overwhelmed
educatorCards.summary: Reduce demand before repeating instructions. Regulation comes before compliance.
steps: Acknowledge the stress without adding urgency.
Offer one clear next step, not a full list.
Reduce the amount of reading or task volume temporarily.
Reconnect the learner with the right adult if the barrier is emotional, not academic.

educatorCards.title: When a learner keeps missing lessons
educatorCards.summary: Treat repeated absence as a support signal as well as an attendance issue.
steps: Find out whether the barrier is access, anxiety, executive function, or misunderstanding.
Send the simplest possible re-entry instruction.
Use pastoral and support-for-learning routes early.
Make the next lesson feel rejoinable, not punitive.

educatorCards.title: When additional needs are in play
educatorCards.summary: Consistency, chunking, and lower cognitive load often matter more than extra explanation.
steps: Use shorter instructions with one action per line.
Offer read-aloud, reduced text load, or visual chunking.
Keep routines predictable and visible.
Check whether the learner needs access adjustments rather than more encouragement.

educatorCards.title: When practical blockers are building up
educatorCards.summary: Small barriers like inbox overload, screen fatigue, or restlessness can quietly stop learning before anyone notices.
steps: Check whether the learner is overwhelmed by emails, tabs, or too many instructions.
Offer a short movement or screen break before re-entry.
Reduce the task to one visible first action.
Keep the message practical and calm so the learner can rejoin quickly.

accessibilityFeatures.title: Chunk the work
accessibilityFeatures.description: Break tasks into smaller visible steps and avoid presenting the whole demand at once.

accessibilityFeatures.title: Offer multiple access routes
accessibilityFeatures.description: Support reading, listening, speaking, and visual structure so the same learning is available in more than one format.

accessibilityFeatures.title: Reduce language load
accessibilityFeatures.description: Prefer short sentences, concrete verbs, and one instruction at a time.

accessibilityFeatures.title: Keep support explicit
accessibilityFeatures.description: Show learners exactly who they can contact and what words they can use when they are stuck.

accessibilityFeatures.title: Build in physical and visual resets
accessibilityFeatures.description: Offer short screen breaks, stretch moments, and one-task re-entry points when attention or regulation is dropping.

footer.title: If today feels hard, start with one small next step.
footer.text: You are still part of school, even on a difficult day. One message, one clarified task, or one check-in is enough to begin again.
footer.note: If you feel stuck, contact your teacher, tutor, or pastoral team so they can help you rejoin.

adultFooter.title: Reduce the barrier first, then rebuild the routine.
adultFooter.text: Adults can help most by making re-entry feel possible: clarify the next step, lower the language load, and reconnect the learner with the right person early.
adultFooter.note: If concern continues, use tutor, pastoral, and support-for-learning routes before the learner falls further behind.
